policy_statement: Plan for the first Ultra-Low Energy (ULE) or Net Zero Energy (NZE) County building.
mechanism_description: The county plans for an ultra-low energy or net-zero energy building, reducing operational energy use and associated greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building design + operations → ↓ energy demand and/or ↑ zero-carbon energy supply → ↓ fossil energy combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Building planning, design, construction, commissioning, and operations are modified to meet ultra-low energy or net-zero energy performance (e.g., high-performance envelope, efficient HVAC/lighting/appliances, controls, on-site renewables or procured clean electricity).  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced thermal losses and improved equipment efficiency lower delivered energy; electrification and renewable/clean electricity reduce combustion-related emissions intensity of remaining energy use.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower operational CO₂e emissions from electricity and/or on-site fuel use over the building lifecycle (with potential co-benefits of reduced peak demand).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building operational greenhouse gas emissions from lower energy use and cleaner energy supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Net-zero/ultra-low-energy buildings reduce operational energy use and/or decarbonize energy supply, resulting in lower GHG emissions—this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism improves energy efficiency (a resource-efficiency co-benefit) and can enhance resilience/comfort (adaptation co-benefit), the largest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is operational emissions reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “net-zero” claim is achieved mainly via off-site offsets/RECs without real operational reductions, the mitigation benefit could be weaker; however, the canonical mechanism specified explicitly targets operational energy/emissions reductions, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy whose mechanism is “net-zero/ultra-low-energy building program → reduced operational emissions” shares the same primary mitigation pathway regardless of jurisdiction or building type.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy is best tagged as efficiency, electrification, renewables, public-procurement, or code-based is policy-specific and requires additional evidence; that finer assignment is deferred to policy-level details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge cases: “Net-zero” achieved via offsets/RECs rather than real energy reductions; embodied carbon from construction could be significant but is not the primary mechanism here; resilience/passive survivability measures may be included but are secondary unless explicitly the main goal.
key_indicators: "Plan for"; "first Ultra-Low Energy (ULE) or Net Zero Energy (NZE) County building"
co_benefits: Operational cost savings; improved indoor comfort/air quality; reduced peak electricity demand
instance_edge_case_notes: Could alternatively be treated as a procurement requirement or performance standard if the underlying policy text specifies mandatory delivery/requirements for the building; here it only states “plan for,” so it is classified as planning-strategy.
